projects.name: Tam-AI-Ti
projects.tagline: AI Financial Wellness Coach grounded in Te Whare Tapa Whā — built solo for Māori financial-prosperity researcher Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa
projects.publicSummary:
AI Financial Wellness Coach for Māori communities, architected on the Te Whare
Tapa Whā holistic-health framework — culture is in the schema, not the UI.
Built solo for Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa (former KPMG NZ National Māori Sector
lead, now at ASB Bank) as an independent research-pilot commission, introduced
via Dr Mahsa McCauley, founder of She Sharp. Voice coaching via GPT-4o
Realtime API, bilingual te reo Māori + English, 88+ AI actions, pgVector
semantic search across 48 database tables. 19-user research cohort produced
181 journal entries, 35 voice sessions, and 146 AI coach messages over four
months (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026).

narrative.problem:
Mainstream FinTech treats money as decontextualized numbers — budgets,
balances, projections. For Māori users, that framing is culturally
mismatched: money is inseparable from whānau wellbeing, spiritual purpose,
and holistic health. Generic budgeting apps strip out the very framework
that makes financial decisions meaningful, and Māori communities — who
face disproportionate financial stress in Aotearoa — are left choosing
between tools that work for their wallets but not their worldview, or no
tools at all. Tam-AI-Ti exists to test whether an AI financial coach built
natively on Māori cultural wisdom — rather than retrofitted with cultural
decoration — can engage a population that mainstream FinTech systematically
underserves.
narrative.audience:
Māori adults engaging with personal financial wellbeing, served along two
account types: individual goal-setters (Full Account) and whānau supporters
who can witness and encourage from the sidelines through a five-tier
permission system (view profile, view goals, view journal, send messages,
receive celebrations). Pilot cohort to date: 19 verified research
participants recruited by Missy Te Kanawa, active over a four-month window
(Oct 2025 – Mar 2026), producing 181 bilingual journal entries, 35 voice
coaching sessions, 146 AI coach messages, and 74 daily Hauora check-ins.
Te reo Māori and English are first-class throughout the experience.
Future audience: the broader Māori adult population — rangatahi entering
working life, parents managing whānau finances, kaumātua passing on
financial wisdom — for whom culturally-rooted tools are scarce.
narrative.productLogic:
The product is architected on Te Whare Tapa Whā, the Māori model of
holistic wellness with four walls (Wairua/spiritual, Tinana/physical,
Hinengaro/mental, Whānau/family) standing on a foundation (Whenua/land).
Every feature traces back to this house.

A user starts with the 26Q assessment mapping nine psychological factors
— locus of control, self-efficacy, resilience, and others — to
personalized recommendations. They set financial goals through one of
seven SMART action plans: reduce spending, increase savings, hit a
savings-rate target, reduce debt, adjust income, review budget, or
partner with someone for accountability. Each plan is tracked with
type-specific fields rather than a generic note column.

They check in daily across eleven Hauora dimensions mapped directly to
the four walls: sleep, water, food, exercise for Tinana; karakia, human
connection, Taiao (environment) connection for Wairua; mindfulness and
learning for Hinengaro; whānau check-in and quality time for Whānau.
They journal morning intentions and evening reflections in te reo or
English, with GPT-4o enhancement and translation.

They meet an AI Financial Coach via GPT-4o Realtime voice — a
conversation partner that never reduces money to numbers but consistently
frames every financial decision through all four walls of the Hauora
model. The AI surfaces Whakatauki (74 curated Māori proverbs) by theme,
references the Maramataka (30-day lunar calendar) for cultural timing,
and ties financial decisions back to family and spiritual purpose.
Whānau members can be invited with the five-tier permission system to
encourage and witness without intruding.
narrative.businessLogic:
Currently a research-pilot product, not yet commercial. Funded directly
by the researcher as an independent contracted build. The theory of
change: if a financial wellness tool authentically reflects Māori
cultural frameworks — rather than skinning generic FinTech with cultural
decoration — it can drive engagement and outcomes for a population that
mainstream products systematically miss.

The hypothesis sits inside Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa's broader body of work
on Māori economic prosperity. She is lead author of KPMG New Zealand's
Maui Rau report series on how Māori can advance prosperity for their
people, has spoken at the National Māori Governance Summit, contributes
to The Spinoff, and brings two decades of experience as a Chartered
Accountant and management consultant. Former Partner at KPMG NZ leading
the National Māori Sector; now at ASB Bank. Tam-AI-Ti is the working
artifact for that thesis — a product proving culturally-native technology
can serve where culturally-decorated technology cannot.

Commercialization paths remain open: iwi partnerships, Māori community
organizations, financial institutions seeking to authentically serve the
Māori sector.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Solo full-stack build on Next.js 15 with Turbopack and TypeScript.
Drizzle ORM over Neon Postgres with pgVector for 1536-dimension semantic
search across goals, journal entries, and coach conversations.
Authentication via Auth.js with the Drizzle adapter.

The AI layer is a deliberate three-model composition chosen for
cost-and-quality fit. CopilotKit drives in-app tool actions on
gpt-4o-mini at temperature 0.3 — deterministic and cheap for tool calls.
GPT-4o full handles high-quality journal enhancement and te reo Māori
translation where prose quality matters. OpenAI's Realtime API
(gpt-4o-realtime-preview, "verse" voice) powers the voice Financial
Coach with server-side VAD and 500ms silence detection. Voice sessions
store text transcripts only — a cost optimization that preserves
searchability without per-minute audio storage.

Culture is in the schema, not the UI. Every one of the 11 Hauora
dimensions is a typed column with a Māori-language identifier
(`wairuaTaiaConnection`, `hinengaro_mauri_tau`, `whanauCheckIn`); the
seven SMART action plans are first-class types `a`–`g` with type-specific
tracking fields rather than a generic JSON blob; the 74 Whakatauki
proverbs are an indexed, embedded library searchable by theme. 48
tables, 494 columns, 76 API routes, 38 pages, 115+ React components.
Edge-runtime endpoints on Vercel.
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed at tamaiti.whiri-ai.com, in active operation since
October 2025. Research cohort engagement (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026,
approximately four months): 19 verified users, 181 bilingual journal
entries, 18 active financial goals, 146 AI coach messages, 35 voice
coaching sessions, 74 daily Hauora check-ins. Sustained multi-month
engagement on a pre-commercial product with no marketing channel — a
meaningful early signal for a research-pilot product. Project status:
fully developed, deployed, and in operating phase. Sits within Missy
Te Kanawa's wider body of published work on Māori economic prosperity
(KPMG Maui Rau report series, Māori Governance Summit talks, The
Spinoff contributing author).
narrative.myRole:
Solo independent developer with full ownership of the entire stack and
lifecycle. Introduced to the project by Dr Mahsa McCauley (Mohaghegh),
founder of She Sharp (New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit) —
Missy approached Mahsa for a developer recommendation and Mahsa referred
me from the She Sharp engineering team, where I serve as Senior Full
Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead.

Engagement model: Missy as client, directing the product structurally
(feature requests, surveys, "include the Maramataka lunar calendar,"
"add a Financial Coach feature") and funding the work directly; I
designed, built, deployed, and maintained the entire system, and ran
the data analysis. Throughout development I sent feature-demo videos at
every milestone so Missy could review progress without needing a
technical background. Post-launch, I produced teaching videos enabling
Missy to use Cursor to do her own database reads and basic data
analysis — transferring practical operational ownership while remaining
the technical lead. 349 commits, solo authorship throughout.

projects.publicSummary:
Solo-built production member platform for She Sharp — New Zealand's
leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (charitable trust, 2,200+ registered
members / 8,000+ women supported lifetime, 50+ corporate partners, 84+
events since 2014). Replaces the previous Webflow site. 648 of 748

AI mentor-mentee matching with five-dimensional scoring + human review,
role-aware mentor / mentee / admin dashboards with dual-role support,

events from the old Webflow site, the Her Waka MSD-funded teaching
site (Mintlify, curriculum authored solo), and a professional SEO
audit on shesharp.org.nz with a 10-point recommendations report.

narrative.problem:
She Sharp is New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit — a
registered charitable trust founded by Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley),
AI/NLP researcher and Director of Women in Tech at AUT, after she
experienced first-hand the gap of being part of the 20% minority of
women in technology. The community has grown to 2,200+ registered
members, 8,000+ women supported across its history, 50+ corporate
partners, and 84+ events since 2014. The original website was a
Webflow build, and Webflow had become the bottleneck: the platform
capabilities the mission required — membership tiers, mentor / mentee
/ admin role permissions, AI-driven mentor matching, Stripe billing,
Slack-integrated operations, automated event management, recruitment
pipelines, audit logging — are precisely what no SaaS site builder
can express. The leadership team was running a 2,200-member community
through spreadsheets, Slack threads, and manual outreach. She Sharp
needed a real platform.
narrative.audience:
Active members of the She Sharp community: women in technology at
every career stage, from undergraduate students considering STEM
majors through senior engineers serving as mentors. The platform
supports three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor, mentee, admin)
plus dual-role compatibility — a mid-career engineer can
simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored by a senior, without
special-cased data modelling. Additional audiences served on adjacent
surfaces: Her Waka programme participants (women re-entering the
workforce, served by the dedicated MSD-funded teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz), volunteer and ambassador candidates
(screened through the AI recruitment pipeline), the 50+ corporate
partners (sponsor packages tied to event visibility and member
access), and She Sharp's operational team (Slack-integrated
workflows). 1,000+ members are actively engaged today, drawn from a
registered base of 2,200+ and a lifetime supported population of
8,000+ women.
narrative.productLogic:
The new platform replaces the old Webflow site with a real member
system anchored on three coexisting dashboards. A user signs up via
email or OAuth, then independently activates any combination of
mentor, mentee, or admin roles — duality is the design principle, not
a special case.

The headline feature is AI-powered mentor-mentee matching, a
two-stage pipeline. Stage one is a rule-based pre-filter (~30-point
threshold) that rapidly scores candidate pairs on basic compatibility.
Stage two passes promising pairs to GPT-4o-mini for evaluation along
five dimensions: professional alignment, skill complementarity,
communication style fit, availability overlap, and growth potential.
Each match returns a 0-100 compatibility score with an AI-generated
natural-language justification. Matches above threshold surface to an
admin for human approval before pairing — conservative human-in-the-
loop automation, not speculative. A mentee matching queue holds
unmatched mentees in priority order when mentor capacity is full,
re-evaluating as slots open. Meetings are typed (intro / regular /
milestone / final), tracked with action items, notes, feedback, and
ratings.

The platform also runs She Sharp's events infrastructure (workshops,
training, networking, social, THRIVE programmes — online, in-person,
or hybrid, with capacity, waitlist, and QR check-in), Stripe-billed
membership tiers (free / basic / premium), a tiered resource library,
transactional email via Resend, and comprehensive audit logging
(JSONB change diffs on every admin action).

A custom Slack ecosystem is deeply integrated: an event-collector
bot, volunteer notification webhooks, a weekly mentorship stats
digest, a weekly NZ funding-opportunities crawler that posts grant
opportunities to a Slack channel, and interactive event handlers.
The most distinctive piece is a Claude agent skill named
`sync-event-from-slack` that automates the previously-manual work of
turning a Slack event channel into a published event page: it reads
channel messages, pinned items, bookmarks, speaker .docx bios, cover
posters, and headshots; downloads images and extracts text; composes
a structured JSON entry; runs a dry-run preview; and on approval
commits the change to git. Event publication has gone from hours of
manual editing to a single command.
narrative.businessLogic:
She Sharp is a registered New Zealand charitable trust. Revenue and
sustainability flow through three channels that the platform now
operationalizes at scale: membership tier subscriptions (free /
basic / premium, via Stripe), 50+ corporate-partner relationships
(sponsor packages tied to event visibility and member-base access),
and grant-funded programmes — notably Her Waka, an NZ Ministry of
Social Development-funded four-session employment-readiness
programme for women re-entering the workforce, run at academyEX
Auckland (March–June 2026), with its own dedicated teaching site
I built and authored.

The platform is the operational substrate that makes each revenue
line scalable: membership growth doesn't proportionally increase
staff load, because dashboards, matching, billing, communications,
recruitment, and event management are automated. The organization's
mission scaling (from the 1,500 active members publicly cited a year
ago, toward the 2,200+ registered base today and beyond) depends
directly on this technical capability.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Next.js 15.4 + React 19.1.2 + TypeScript over Drizzle ORM on Neon
Postgres, authenticated via NextAuth 5 with bcrypt and OAuth providers,
deployed on Vercel's edge network with documented sub-second TTFB.
Tailwind 4 + shadcn/ui + Radix primitives for the UI.

The schema runs to 1,392 lines and 45 enums — a deliberate choice.
Membership tiers, mentorship-relationship states (pending / active /
paused / completed / rejected), meeting types (intro / regular /
milestone / final), event types, career stages (undergraduate →
postgraduate → early_career → mid_career → senior → career_transition),
MBTI personality types, recruitment stages — every business concept
is a typed enum rather than a string column, so invalid states are
unrepresentable.

The AI matching engine combines a deterministic rule-based pre-filter
with GPT-4o-mini for high-potential pairs only — keeping cost
predictable and outputs explainable, with admin override as the final
gate. Slack integration uses scoped read-only tokens plus targeted
webhooks. The `sync-event-from-slack` agent skill composes existing
CLI tools (Slack fetch, image download, docx parse, JSON validate,
git commit) into a single intent-driven workflow with dry-run preview
and CI gating. Image storage on Cloudinary, transactional email via
Resend, payments via Stripe.

The role spans beyond the main platform. Three supporting projects
round out the contribution. **she-sharp-crawl** — a Python crawler
(BeautifulSoup4, Requests) that migrated content from the old Webflow
site: 1,748 images totaling 707 MB across 11 modular JSON files,
covering 85 SheSharp events, 50 Humanitix ticketed events (with banner
images upgraded 500 px → 1,600 px), 15 podcasts, 121 photo galleries,
7 news / press articles, and 118 site pages. Image deduplication and
Wayback Machine fallback for dead links. **her-waka** — the Mintlify-
powered teaching site at herwaka.shesharp.org.nz for the MSD-funded
Her Waka programme; I authored both the platform and the curriculum
(four workshops covering AI & Future of Work, #IAmRemarkable + Vibe
Coding, Cybersecurity, Personal Branding, plus 8+ self-paced
tutorials at three difficulty tiers). **serpdata-api-test** — a
professional SEO audit on shesharp.org.nz built on Node 22 native
fetch over the SerpData API, running ~20 live Google queries across
eight analysis sections (brand visibility, topical keyword rankings,
NZ competitor matrix, mobile-vs-desktop gap, cross-market reach,
SERP feature inventory) plus a 10-point prioritized recommendations
report (e.g., "SheSharp ranks #4 desktop / not in mobile top 10 for
'women in tech New Zealand' — high-priority technical SEO gap").
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed and operating She Sharp's full member lifecycle.
Commit volume: **748 total commits, 648 (86.6%) authored solo by
Chan Meng**, with the remaining ~100 commits by Lesley Gao on
frontend design and code. The platform now runs She Sharp's
membership, mentorship, events, sponsor relationships, volunteer
recruitment pipeline, and internal team operations — the technical
infrastructure for an organization with 2,200+ registered members,
1,000+ active, 8,000+ women supported lifetime, 50+ corporate
partners, 84+ events since 2014, 18 industry mentors, and a reported
85% mentorship success rate (mentees achieve 5-6× higher promotion
likelihood per She Sharp's own outcomes data).

The Her Waka programme launched in March 2026 with MSD funding, using
the dedicated teaching site I built and the curriculum I authored.
The SEO audit identified actionable competitive gaps — notably the
desktop/mobile ranking disparity on the core query "women in tech
New Zealand" — and informed near-term content strategy. The
migration crawler enabled a clean cutover from Webflow without
losing institutional knowledge.
narrative.myRole:
Senior Full Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead. Recruited directly
by founder Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley) — AI/NLP researcher and
Director of Women in Tech at AUT — specifically to deliver the AI
and full-stack senior engineering that Webflow couldn't, and that
the She Sharp team didn't have in-house.

Solo full-stack ownership of the main platform: product architecture,
database schema (1,392 lines, 45 enums), AI matching pipeline, Slack
ecosystem, agent skills, Stripe integration, admin systems,
deployments, observability, and operational support. Lesley Gao
contributed some frontend design and frontend code (~100 commits,
13.4% of total); the remaining 648 commits (86.6%) are mine. Beyond
the main platform I also designed and built the data migration
crawler that bootstrapped the launch (she-sharp-crawl), the Her Waka
teaching site (her-waka) and its complete four-workshop + 8-tutorial
curriculum, and the professional SEO audit and recommendations
report (serpdata-api-test) on shesharp.org.nz — operating across
engineering, instructional design, and strategic analysis under one
role.

projects.publicSummary:
AI-orchestrated post-app activation platform for GAVIGO Inc. (Delaware
C-Corp; founder & CEO Saba Gecgil). Public mission: "Skip the Store.
Start the Play" — eliminate the latency between user intent and
interactive content delivery. Distributed system: Go orchestrator
(WebSocket hub + Scorer + 7-trigger rules engine + Kubernetes client),
React dashboard for live orchestration visualization, React Native /
Expo mobile app with TikTok-style feed. Originally architected and
shipped on **DigitalOcean managed Kubernetes (DOKS, Singapore)**, then
migrated to **Google Cloud GKE (Belgium)** in a single ~30-minute
production cutover (2026-03-11) when GAVIGO's Google Cloud partnership
came into effect — production expertise on both clouds. Real measured
performance post-migration: restore path <1 ms p50, cold path ~12 ms,
84.6% warm-pool hit rate. 360 total commits, 353 (98%) solo. As
Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems, I
own the contractually-defined "Protected Technical Domain": activation
systems, activation flow and execution behavior, activation-economics
proof path, AI-assisted orchestration and execution-control,
restore/reuse boundaries, and heavier-workload validation.

narrative.problem:
The native-app paradigm puts a friction tax on every interactive
digital experience: discover an app → app-store landing page → install
→ onboard → first interaction. By the time the user reaches the thing
they actually wanted, the moment has passed. For games, video, and
AI experiences — where perception of instantaneity drives engagement
— the install gap is the single largest leak in the funnel. GAVIGO's
thesis (in its own marketing language): we are building the
"Post-App World," eliminating the trillion-dollar friction between
user intent and digital experience through proprietary "Instant
Reality Switching" technology.
narrative.audience:
Two surfaces in production today. **Consumer surface**: end users on
ire.gavigo.com and the mobile build, scrolling a TikTok-style
vertical feed of FOSS games (Hextris, Clumsy Bird, Tiny Yurts,
Catculus, Casual Crusade, Stolen Sword, and others — 9 vendored to
date from a curated set of 11 open-source repos analyzed). **Investor
/ operator surface**: the React dashboard at the same domain, which
shows the orchestrator's AI decisions, container state machine
(COLD → PREPARING → READY → ACTIVE → REUSABLE), warm-pool budget,
activation-quality metrics, and side-by-side iframe of the mobile
app — purpose-built for live demos to investors and partner
evaluators. A Phase 9 audience-segmentation feature filters
visibility per role (public / investor / operator presets) at
WebSocket broadcast time. The ultimate addressable surface is any
market where perception of instantaneity drives engagement — mobile
games, short-form video, AI chat, generative content.
narrative.productLogic:
A user scrolls a TikTok-style vertical feed; the orchestrator
watches focus events stream over a WebSocket hub, scores each
candidate piece of content on three weighted signals (personal
attention 0.4, global attention 0.4, trend / virality 0.2), and
when combined score crosses a 0.6 threshold, fires one of seven AI
triggers — Swarm Boost, Proactive Warm, Cross-Domain Inject,
Mode Change, Lookahead Warm, Initial Warm, Checkpoint Restore —
each of which patches Kubernetes deployment replicas through the
K8s client-go API to scale a game container to READY before the
user taps.

When the user taps, the experience is already live — either served
instantly from a single always-warm shared game-server pod
(~12 ms cold path) or restored from the Reusable pool of recently-
active containers (<1 ms restore path). State survives across
scrolls via a postMessage protocol on web and MMKV storage on
native; both reconcile against Redis as a single source of truth
through a deterministic five-state Activation Spine
(COLD → READY → ACTIVE → REUSABLE → restore). When mobile gaming
meets the install gap, the GAVIGO answer is: there is no install
gap.
narrative.businessLogic:
GAVIGO Inc. is a Delaware-incorporated C-Corp at the pre-Series A
stage, founder Saba Gecgil. The product wedge is mobile gaming
(public tagline: "Skip the Store. Start the Play") — but the
underlying activation engine is platform-shaped: any interactive
content category where the install gap or cold-start latency is
the dominant funnel leak. The defensibility is architectural,
not feature-level: a proprietary Activation Spine plus a measured
sub-millisecond restore path is the kind of capability that
compounds, doesn't decay. GAVIGO frames its architecture publicly
as "Two Pillars": the Technology Platform (CTO-led foundation)
and the Intelligence Layer (the "soul" — the autonomous predictive
engine that makes Instant Reality possible). I own the
Intelligence Layer side of that architecture under the title
Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The orchestrator is a single Go binary (gorilla/websocket hub +
Scorer + Rules Engine + K8s client-go patcher) deployed to a
managed Kubernetes cluster. Companion pods: a React + Vite
dashboard, a React Native / Expo mobile-web build served from an
nginx pod, a single always-warm nginx game-server pod baking ~25
FOSS games (zero per-game startup latency), an AI-service pod
(Node + Express + GPT-4o-mini) that scales 0→1, Phase 6+ compute
/ inference workloads, Redis 7 (allkeys-LRU eviction, 400 MB
budget) for warm-pool state and activation metrics, and optional
PostgreSQL 16 for social data.

**Cross-cloud production experience: DigitalOcean and Google Cloud.**
The platform shipped first on **DigitalOcean Kubernetes (DOKS) in
Singapore (sgp1)** — DO Container Registry, DO LoadBalancer
(REGIONAL type), Let's Encrypt with manual certbot renewal — chosen
for cost-efficient prototype-to-production iteration. When GAVIGO's
Google Cloud partnership came into effect, I led the full migration
to **GKE Standard Zonal in Belgium (europe-west1-b)** on 2026-03-11.
The cutover rebuilt the entire infrastructure stack: DO Container
Registry → Artifact Registry in europe-west3 (cross-region pulls
within GCP); DO LoadBalancer → GCE Ingress + Global HTTP(S) LB
with Google-managed certs (auto-renewal replacing 80-day certbot
toil); DO LB redirect annotation → FrontendConfig
`redirectToHttps: true`; DO LB default WebSocket timeouts →
BackendConfig `timeoutSec: 86400` to keep activation WS sessions
alive; service types reshaped from LoadBalancer-per-frontend to
ClusterIP-behind-Ingress; static IP swapped to a GCP global static
IP. All five deployments (orchestrator, frontend, mobile-web,
ai-service, redis) migrated cleanly with `strategy: Recreate` to
avoid CPU deadlock on single-node clusters. Total production
downtime during cutover: **~30 minutes** (DNS propagation + cert
provisioning). The migration is fully documented in the project's
`docs/archive/gcp-migration-record.md` for institutional memory.

Hard problems solved. **Sub-millisecond restore** through a
deterministic five-state Activation Spine with atomic Redis
transitions and phase-by-phase timestamps that prove the
decision → K8s patch → client observation chain. **Warm-pool
LRU without cascade eviction** via ProtectSlot reservations that
keep controlled / benchmark games safe from displacement.
**Cross-platform state sync** between MMKV (native), localStorage
/ postMessage (web), and Redis (server) with a typed game
protocol bridging iframe ↔ WebView. **Async K8s coordination
with real-time telemetry** — pod EndpointSlice / Pod-status
watches feed activation_ready broadcasts with millisecond-
precision timestamps. **Audience-aware broadcast filtering**
(Phase 9) where the WebSocket hub filters event envelopes per
client preset (public / investor / operator) so each viewer sees
a different slice of the catalog. **Benchmark spine** running
20 iterations per path on real K8s, validating <1 ms restore p50
and ~12 ms cold p50 against load-tested 100 concurrent WebSocket
clients.

Engineering culture is phase-driven with comprehensive proof
packs: each major capability lands as a phase (Phase 1 init →
Phase 2 telemetry → Phase 3 shared game-server → Phase 4 service
worker / iframe pool → Phase 5 activation spine → Phase 6
compute workloads → Phase 7-8 proof pack → Phase 9 audience
presets), with documentation and benchmark numbers required to
promote work to public surfaces.
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed at ire.gavigo.com. First shipped on
DigitalOcean DOKS (Singapore), then migrated to Google Cloud GKE
(Belgium) on 2026-03-11 in a clean ~30-minute cutover when GAVIGO's
Google Cloud partnership came into effect — production-grade
expertise demonstrated on both clouds. 360 total commits, 353 solo
(98%, across both my git identities); remainder GitHub Actions bot.
Real measured platform metrics post-migration: restore path <1 ms
p50, cold path ~12 ms p50, 84.6% warm-pool hit rate, 0 errors at
100 concurrent WebSocket clients in load tests. Catalog: 9 vendored
FOSS games (curated from 11 open-source repos analyzed for license,
build size, and web-first compatibility) across 9 studios, 199
bundled videos. Phase 9 audience-segmentation live: investors and
operators see different catalogs than the public surface.
Pre-Series A; product is the working artifact for upcoming
investor conversations. Role formally formalized over three
contract iterations (Original Oct 17 2025; Amended Feb 9 2026;
Second Amended Apr 16 2026), each escalating recognition of my
technical ownership area.

**External validation cycle, Feb 2026:** GAVIGO was accepted
into the **Google for Startups Cloud Program** — announced by
founder Saba Gecgil with the message that the value isn't cloud
credits but the ability to "test [our] assumptions in depth and
prove that faster activation and higher concurrency don't
require more hardware, just better control over how compute is
used." That partnership is what unlocked the GCP migration I
executed on March 11. The same window brought acceptance into
the **NVIDIA Inception Program** — the deep-tech / GPU
infrastructure track that supports startups building GPU-
intensive infrastructure-level technologies. And the company
was profiled by tech-business press at feedvoice.com on
2026-02-10 in *"How GAVIGO Is Ending App Store Friction for
Instant Digital Play"* — the public account names the
sub-0.2-second activation claim, the patented execution-layer
IP positioning, the "Watch and Play era" narrative, and the
cross-border Istanbul-to-global ambition.
narrative.myRole:
Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems

Joined Oct 17 2025; current title and scope formalized by the

The contract defines a "Protected Technical Domain" that anchors

Recognized in the contract record as "a core long-term technical

projects.publicSummary:
An early-ecosystem MCP server — first commit **Dec 30 2024, just
35 days after Anthropic's public launch of the Model Context
Protocol on Nov 25 2024**. Brings Google News search to AI
assistants like Claude Desktop, Cline, and Cursor through SerpAPI,
with automatic topical categorization (AI & Technology, Business,
Science & Research, Healthcare, General) and multi-language /
multi-region support. Carries the **PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge**
with 26.1k estimated visitors and a #1,105 global popularity rank.
Featured in three independent deep-dive write-ups: Skywork AI's
*"Chan Meng's Google News MCP Server: The AI Engineer's Guide to
Real-Time News Integration"*, Skywork's *"Google News & Trends
MCP Server: The AI Engineer's Deep Dive"*, and AgentSkillsHub's
*"Install Guide & Security Review"*. Distributed across **15+
MCP catalogs and registries** — Smithery, mcp.so, Glama (A
license rating, 122 stars), PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers,
MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive, Playbooks, mcpservers.org,
Archestra, serp.ai, mcp-archive.com, AIBase, AgentSkillsHub,
MagicSlides — and npm-published as
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`. Paired with a companion early
server, `server-google-jobs` (first commit Dec 31 2024) —
together 169 solo commits across the two servers. Beyond shipping
code, I contributed early ecosystem troubleshooting via
[modelcontextprotocol/servers issue #76](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers/issues/76)
(opened Nov 27 2024) — the Windows + NVM PATH workaround
referenced in both server READMEs as primary troubleshooting
guidance.

narrative.problem:
Anthropic published the Model Context Protocol specification in
November 2024 as the open standard for connecting AI assistants
like Claude to external tools and data sources — but in the first
weeks after launch, the ecosystem had only a handful of reference
servers and no real way for AI assistants to search current news.
Generative engines (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity) were increasingly
being asked "what's happening with X today" — questions an LLM
cannot answer from training data alone. The gap was concrete:
Claude Desktop users wanted current-events search; the MCP server
catalog had nothing for Google News.

A second, related friction surfaced almost immediately: Windows
users running Node Version Manager couldn't get any npm-based MCP
server (filesystem, github, postgres, brave-search, google-maps,
etc.) to connect to Claude Desktop, because PATH resolution
through NVM's shim layer broke the desktop client's process
spawn. This blocked a large fraction of early Windows adopters
from using MCP at all.
narrative.audience:
AI assistants and the developers integrating them. Direct
consumers: Claude Desktop, Cline, Cursor's agent mode, Continue,
any MCP-compliant client running a system prompt that includes
news-search capability. Downstream consumers: end-users asking
their AI assistant about current events, journalists / analysts
using AI workflows to monitor coverage, developers building
research agents and content workflows. The multi-registry
distribution surface (10+ catalogs) was designed for *discovery*:
AI developers find MCP servers by browsing registries, not by
searching GitHub.
narrative.productLogic:
A user asks their AI assistant something like "what's happening
with [topic] in [region] this week"; the assistant invokes the
MCP server's `google_news_search` tool with `query`, optional
`gl` (country code), and `hl` (language code) parameters; the
server relays the request to SerpAPI's `google_news` engine and
returns structured JSON; the server then post-processes the
results with regex-based topic categorization (AI & Technology,
Business, Science & Research, Healthcare, General) and formats
the output with source attribution, dates, snippets, and links
intact.

The companion `server-google-jobs` follows the same pattern with
a richer parameter set — `query` plus `location`, `posted_age`
(today / 3 days / week / month), `employment_type`
(FULLTIME / PARTTIME / CONTRACTOR / INTERN), `salary`, `radius`,
`hl`, `page`, `sort_by` — and ships with **full localization
for English, Simplified Chinese, Japanese, and Korean**
(30+ translated strings per locale).

Both servers run over MCP's `stdio` transport — the default
channel for desktop AI clients — and are installable in one
command via Smithery's CLI:
`npx -y @smithery/cli install @chanmeng666/google-news-server --client claude`.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source, MIT licensed, no direct monetization. The strategic
intent is **ecosystem positioning**: be the canonical Google
News / Google Jobs MCP server during the formative months of the
Model Context Protocol so the project compounds discovery for
adjacent work. Multi-registry distribution (Smithery, mcp.so,
Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP Servers, mcpservers.org, MCP
Archive, MCP Server Finder, Playbooks, Archestra) makes the
project surface anywhere a developer looks for an MCP tool.
The README's structured JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` metadata
and `<script type="text/llms.txt">` section take the same
approach to LLM-driven discovery that the rest of my work uses:
AI agents themselves should be able to read the server's
identity, parameters, and use cases without parsing prose.
narrative.technicalApproach:
TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`
v1.0.0 (Google News) and v1.25.1 (Google Jobs — the newer SDK
pin reflects ongoing maintenance). ES module target, packaged
as a CLI binary (`google-news-server`, `google-jobs-server`)
exported in `package.json`. Transport is stdio — the default
channel for desktop AI clients, where the server runs as a
spawned subprocess of Claude Desktop or equivalent. HTTP is
handled with axios on the News server and node-fetch on the
Jobs server (deliberately leaner — no axios, no dotenv
overhead, environment read directly from `process.env`).

SerpAPI ($75/mo paid tier for 5,000 News searches, $50/mo for
5,000 Jobs searches; 2 req/sec rate limit, 5 concurrent, 1 h
cache) is the underlying data plane. The servers add a thin
intelligence layer on top: query parameter validation,
categorization post-processing, multi-language formatting,
error handling with localized messages. Both READMEs are 320-335
lines and structured with badges, video demos, MseeP.ai
security badges, JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` schema, and an
explicit "AI Assistant Instructions & GEO Optimization"
collapsible section — the same GEO-aware authoring pattern I
use across all my work.

Beyond shipping code, the third technical contribution is
community: my **issue #76** (opened Nov 27 2024, three weeks
after MCP's launch) documented the Windows + NVM `PATH`
resolution failure that blocked Claude Desktop from spawning
npm-based MCP servers. The fix — point Claude Desktop's config
at the absolute path of `node.exe` rather than relying on PATH
— is referenced as primary troubleshooting guidance in both my
own server READMEs and (per the README's own claim) became
guidance in the broader MCP ecosystem.
narrative.outcomes:
Both servers are production-deployed via npm and listed across
**15+ MCP discovery surfaces**, with independent third-party
validation that this is treated as portfolio-grade MCP work:

**Catalog distribution & quality signals (server-google-news,
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`):**
- **PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge** + 26.1k estimated visitors,
  #1,105 global popularity rank
  ([listing](https://www.pulsemcp.com/servers/chanmeng666-google-news))
- **Glama A-grade license rating** + 122 stars
  ([listing](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/@ChanMeng666/server-google-news))

projects.name: github-readme-suno-cards
projects.tagline: Display your Suno AI music as dynamic, animated SVG cards
projects.publicSummary:
Display your Suno AI music as dynamic, animated SVG cards in your GitHub
README — gallery + builder. Two layouts (classic, player), two colour
presets, three theme modes, eleven element toggles, hex colour overrides;
bilingual CJK-friendly text via SVG `<foreignObject>` plus CSS keyframe
animation on inline HTML spans.

narrative.problem:
Musicians using Suno AI have no elegant way to surface their
catalogue on GitHub profiles or developer portfolios. Either they
link out (and lose the visual hook), screenshot the player (and
lose every update), or hand-edit Markdown (and re-do it every
time they publish a new track). None of these scale beyond a
single song.
narrative.audience:
Developer-musicians using Suno AI who want their GitHub README,
portfolio site, or documentation to display a live, auto-updating
catalogue of their generated music — including CJK-named tracks
and animated visual indicators rather than static thumbnails.
narrative.productLogic:
Two paths. (1) Direct embedding: paste a card URL containing the
Suno song UUID into Markdown — zero setup. (2) GitHub Action
sync: a scheduled workflow fetches the user's public Suno
catalogue, sorts it, and writes a grid of cards between
configurable markers in the README. The hosted builder at
`/builder` provides a live-preview editor over layout, preset,
theme, and element toggles, generating the embed URL for direct
copy-paste.
narrative.technicalApproach:
pnpm monorepo split into three layers: (a) **Suno API parser**
using Valibot schemas — strict TypeScript validation against
the public `studio-api-prod.suno.com` oEmbed endpoints (no
scraping, no auth, no rate-limit landmines); (b) **SVG rendering
engine** built as pure functions so renders are deterministic
and cacheable; (c) **Next.js 15 frontend** deployed on Vercel
Edge Runtime. The interesting rendering trick: SVG
`<foreignObject>` carries inline HTML `<span>` elements so CJK
text wraps and shapes correctly, with CSS `@keyframes`
animations applied to the spans to drive the equaliser bars —
a layout that pure SVG can't produce cleanly. Two-level caching:
Vercel Data Cache upstream (3600s for single cards) and HTTP
`Cache-Control` headers downstream, letting GitHub's Camo proxy
manage the SVG distribution layer. Builder UI: 11 element
toggles, hex colour overrides, two layouts (classic / player),
two presets (default purple / Suno navy-gold), three theme
modes (auto / dark / light).
narrative.outcomes:
Live production service at github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app
with gallery and builder routes. MIT licensed for broad reuse.
Build pipeline is esbuild + CI verification + dual render modes
(live service or local pre-rendering for users who want full
control). Roadmap: waveform variants, lyrics excerpts, playlist
cards, PNG export via `@resvg/resvg`.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the monorepo split, wrote the Valibot
schemas against Suno's oEmbed contract, engineered the
`<foreignObject>` + CSS-keyframes rendering technique, and
built the live builder UI plus the GitHub Action distribution
path.

projects.name: echook (Echo + Hook)
projects.tagline: AI-operated audio notification system for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI — install, configure, and use with natural language only
projects.publicSummary:
AI-operated audio notification system for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and
Codex CLI. The defining product idea: the **human never learns the
tool**. The user talks to their AI agent in plain English ("install
audio-hooks", "switch to chimes", "snooze for 30 minutes", "send alerts
to my Slack"), and the agent runs every shell command, reads every JSON
output, and reports back. Install reduces to **1 shell command + 2
natural-language prompts + 1 slash command = 4 things**; everything
after that is conversation. Ships 26 hook events, 2 audio themes,
rate-limit alerts (5h/7d quota windows), webhooks (Slack/Discord/Teams/
ntfy), TTS that speaks Claude's actual reply, and a context-window
status line. 63 GitHub stars, 42 releases, 139 unittest cases on
Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9–3.13. A sister repo,
**echook-promo-video**, ships the 88-second 1080p product film built
entirely in code (Remotion + Claude Code + ElevenLabs + Suno).
Carved out as Chan's preserved IP in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter (May 2026).

narrative.problem:
Developers running long-form AI coding workflows on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no way to *hear* what their agent
is doing — they alt-tab to check progress, miss rate-limit
warnings, and only discover stuck sessions on visual inspection.
Bolt-on notification tools exist but they all carry the same
anti-pattern: **the human is downstream of the CLI**. The user
has to read docs, memorise flags, copy commands, and re-learn the
same surface every time it changes. That model collapses the
moment a serious user starts rotating between Claude Code, Cursor,
and Codex — three editors, three configuration languages, three
sets of muscle memory. The mismatch isn't audio; it's the
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.
narrative.audience:
Software engineers operating Claude Code / Cursor IDE / Codex CLI
at the "AI-first" level — comfortable telling their coding agent
to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure infrastructure
in plain English rather than memorising subcommands. Also: power
users debugging long-running workflows where audio cues are faster
than visual polling; teams rolling out shared notification
defaults across multiple developers; researchers building on the
AI-first plugin contract as a reference implementation.
narrative.productLogic:
echook inverts the relationship: **the human is upstream of
Claude Code, the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf.**
A one-line install prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — kicks off the entire flow. Claude
Code runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the only
manual slash command in the entire lifetime of the tool, because
Claude Code's reload has no CLI equivalent), and from that moment
forward every interaction is conversation. *"Switch to chimes."*
*"Snooze for 8 hours."* *"Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook
URL and test it."* *"Disable everything except stop and
permission_request."* The `/audio-hooks` skill parses intent,
invokes the right `audio-hooks` CLI subcommand, reads its JSON
output, and narrates the result. The user memorises **nothing**.
Cross-editor parity is part of the same contract: every prompt
works identically on Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI because they
all drive the same Python binary against the same JSON API.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no telemetry.
The business value is **build-in-public credibility**: echook is
the demonstration artifact for Chan's AI-first product philosophy
and shows up in every Engram-cohort interview, every recruiter
conversation, and the public README of every downstream client
project. The 42-release cadence, the cross-platform CI matrix,
and the 139-test bridge-contract suite make it visible production
engineering — not a weekend script. The Remotion promo video
sister repo extends the same proof: it shows Chan can ship not
just the product but the marketing artifact for the product, end
to end, programmatically, with zero Adobe-suite licensing or
third-party video production overhead.
narrative.technicalApproach:
AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` CLI ships a
stable JSON-only API with **27 subcommands, no prompts, no
spinners**, versioned NDJSON event logging (schema
`audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and a **stable error
code enum** (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`, `WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`,
`DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, etc.) where each error carries a
`suggested_command` field so an agent can recover autonomously
without screen-scraping. Hook coverage is **26 events** spanning
session start/end, tool execution, permission requests, file
changes, worktree creation, elicitation, and rate-limit warnings
(5-hour and 7-day quota windows with configurable 80%/95%
thresholds; each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` fires exactly
once via debounce markers). Two audio themes: ElevenLabs
**Jessica** voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are declarative
via `config/audio_manifest.json` + the ElevenLabs generator.
Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy via
fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload — they never block the hook.
The status line ships **10 customisable segments** including
color-coded context-window and API-quota bars with absolute
token counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they enter it. Cross-editor architecture
is the deepest piece: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— **one source of truth, three editor targets**, with CI
enforcing in-sync via `build-plugin.sh --check`. The Cursor path
leverages Cursor 3.2.16+'s auto-bridge so 99% of users get
Cursor support for free; Cursor-only installs go through a
native `~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration with a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error guard that aborts if both paths would
be active. The Codex CLI path goes further: install handles
OpenAI's required `[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` feature flag
by **authoring a fresh `~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists**
and emitting machine-readable `next_steps` for the calling AI
agent to add the flag with its Edit tool when one does — we
never round-trip user TOML because that destroys comments and
formatting. **18 canonical events with no Codex equivalent
no-op cleanly under the codex invoker** with a debug NDJSON
event, never blocking the session. The whole bridge contract is
validated by 139 unittest cases across Ubuntu/Windows/macOS ×
Python 3.9/3.12/3.13 in GitHub Actions CI.
narrative.outcomes:
63 GitHub stars, 6 forks, **42 releases** shipped, **139 unittest
cases**. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan 2026) shipped
the AI-first redesign — JSON CLI, `/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON
logging, plugin-native install; v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native
Codex CLI support with +33 bridge-contract tests; v5.2.1 (May
2026) executed the **echook rebrand** — a "door-only" GitHub
rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` → `echook` (because leading
with "Claude Code" became misleading once native Cursor and
Codex paths shipped) that preserved every existing install via
GitHub's repo redirect, kept the `audio-hooks` CLI name, the
`chanmeng-audio-hooks` marketplace name, and all state
directories unchanged. The Engram Architect Cohort Engagement
Letter (May 9, 2026) explicitly names
*claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's preserved IP — formal
recognition by a paid agentic infrastructure firm that this is
portfolio-grade engineering, not a hobby script. The sister
repo **echook-promo-video** then closed the loop on the
AI-first thesis: an 88.7-second 1920×1080 @ 30fps product film
composed of 9 sections (Intro → Problem → Solution → Features →
Advanced → Demo → Ecosystem → Cross-Platform → CTA) connected
by `@remotion/transitions` (wipe/fade/slide) with a layered
audio mix (Suno-generated BGM with auto volume ducking + 8
ElevenLabs SFX cues + 3 ElevenLabs voiceover tracks) — built
entirely in React + Remotion + AI-generated audio, **zero
Adobe Premiere, zero After Effects, zero human voice actors**.
The whole video pipeline is reproducible: one
`npm run generate-audio` regenerates every voice and effect from
the ElevenLabs API; one `npm run render` produces the MP4.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON API + NDJSON log + stable error codes +
`suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronisation
pipeline (canonical → plugin layout sync via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML-feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the 9-section narrative arc with Claude Code, built
the reusable visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal,
SoundWave, GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated
the Remotion `TransitionSeries`, wrote the ElevenLabs
audio-generation script, and tuned the volume-ducking
envelopes so BGM ducks during VO + demo playback. Maintains
both repos today through the 5.x line.

projects.name: TikTok Shop Go Template
projects.tagline: Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template — clean architecture, JWT auth, bilingual; distilled from ByteDance Youth Training Camp learnings
projects.publicSummary:
Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template
with clean architecture, JWT auth, and paired React frontend — built
solo as a teaching artefact from the ByteDance Youth Training Camp
capstone. 52 GitHub stars, 7 forks, < 50 ms average API response time.

narrative.problem:
Developers learning Go web development have access to plenty of
toy hello-world tutorials and plenty of opaque production
codebases, but very little **in between** — no template that
demonstrates a complete e-commerce backend with the directory
layout, build pipeline, and documentation discipline a Chinese
big-tech team would actually ship.

Two layered audiences. Primary: Go beginners progressing from
toy projects to "ship-shaped" architecture — students from the
ByteDance Youth Training Camp and similar bootcamps. Secondary:
experienced developers needing a clean starter for an
e-commerce backend who don't want to assemble Gin + MyBatis +

Full stack: **Go 1.23 + Gin** for the backend, **MySQL 8.0** for
persistence, **React 18** + Vite for the frontend, **JWT
authentication**, **RESTful APIs**, **Redis** caching. Directory
layout follows the Go community's production-correct convention:
`cmd/server/` (entry points), `internal/` (core business logic),
`api/v1/` (handlers and route definitions), `pkg/` (reusable

HTML/JavaScript version side by side, so learners can compare
architectural approaches against the same backend. Deployment
guides cover Docker, Railway, Render, and Fly.io. Bilingual

**52 GitHub stars, 7 forks**, 27 sustained commits — the

catalogue under "primary" tier. Featured as one of the eight

Doubles as the worked-example artefact accompanying Chan's

Sole author. Designed the directory layout, wrote both backend

projects.name: server-google-jobs
projects.tagline: Companion early-MCP Google Jobs server (Dec 31 2024, 36 days post-MCP-launch) — subject of dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive review, 4-language localized, listed across 10+ MCP catalogs

metrics.label: First commit
metrics.value: 2024-12-31 — 36 days after MCP's Nov 25 2024 public launch

metrics.label: Skywork review
metrics.value: "one of the most elegant, production-ready solutions" (Oct 27 2025)

projects.publicSummary:
MCP server for Google Jobs multi-language search (English, Simplified
Chinese, Japanese, Korean — 30+ translated strings per locale). Built
**Dec 31 2024, 36 days after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 launch of the
Model Context Protocol** — companion to `server-google-news` (one
day earlier). **The subject of a dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive
review** *("The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's Google Jobs MCP
Server"*, Oct 27 2025) that calls it *"one of the most elegant,
production-ready solutions I've found for a very specific, very
common problem"* and benchmarks it favourably against generic
SerpAPI wrappers, custom scrapers, and manual API integrations.
Listed on **PulseMCP** (4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025),
**Glama** (A license rating), Smithery, mcp.so, AI Toolhouse,
AIBase, MagicSlides, and serp.ly — 10+ catalogs total.
Published as `@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server` on npm; installable
through Smithery (`npx -y @smithery/cli install
@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server --client claude`); ships a
Dockerfile and a `.mcpb` compiled binary for frictionless install
on the MCP Registry; security badges from MseeP.ai and Glama.ai.

narrative.problem:
At launch (Dec 31 2024), MCP was weeks old as a public protocol
and there was no canonical way for an AI assistant to query
**multilingual job listings**. Job seekers in non-English markets
— particularly Greater China, Japan, and Korea — couldn't ask
Claude Desktop for relevant openings without falling back to
manually translating queries and hand-pasting URL parameters.
narrative.audience:
Three groups. (a) Developers integrating job search into AI
applications. (b) Multilingual job-seekers using Claude Desktop
and similar MCP-aware tools. (c) Other early-era MCP server
authors wanting a reference implementation of locale-aware
third-party API integration — explicitly recognised in
Skywork AI's *"The Ultimate Guide"* review as a production-grade
reference pattern in the early MCP ecosystem.
narrative.productLogic:
The server exposes Google Jobs (via SerpAPI) as a composable MCP
capability. Clients call `search` with parameters `query`,
`location`, `employment_type`, `salary`, `radius`, `sort_by` —
and a `locale` parameter that maps onto English / Simplified
Chinese / Japanese / Korean. The server returns up to 10 results
per page with all error messages, status labels, and suggestion
prompts translated into the requested locale (30+ strings each).
Input is locale-agnostic: a user writing "part-time" or "兼职"
or "アルバイト" all map to the same SerpAPI employment code.
Skywork's review specifically highlights the **AI-optimized
structured output** as eliminating downstream parsing work and
the **smart fallback mechanisms** as production-ready error
handling.
narrative.technicalApproach:
TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk
1.25.1` (kept current with the official SDK release line). The
hardest design problem was **locale-agnostic parameter
handling** — rather than hard-coding language-specific branches,
the server maintains a 4-locale translation matrix keyed
against canonical SerpAPI codes, so input in any supported
language collapses to the same internal query and output
rehydrates into the user's chosen locale. Distribution surface
is unusually wide for an early MCP: **npm**
(`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`), **Smithery** (one-command
install via `npx -y @smithery/cli`), a **Dockerfile** for
containerised deployment, a `.mcpb` compiled binary for
frictionless install on the official MCP Registry, and
`mcp-evals 2.0.0` as an early adopter of the MCP evaluation
framework. The `package.json` carries a structured `ai:` block
with use cases, capabilities, target audience, and primary use
cases declared in JSON for LLM-driven discovery — the same
GEO-aware authoring pattern used across all of Chan's MCP work.
narrative.outcomes:
**71 solo commits**. Registered on the official MCP Registry;
security-assessment badges from MseeP.ai and Glama.ai validating
runtime reliability. Listed across **10+ MCP discovery
surfaces** — PulseMCP (4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025),
Glama (A license rating), Smithery, mcp.so, AI Toolhouse,
AIBase, MagicSlides, serp.ly, MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive.
**Subject of an independently authored deep-dive review** by
Skywork AI (Oct 27 2025) — *"The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's
Google Jobs MCP Server"* — which calls it *"one of the most
elegant, production-ready solutions"* and includes a
comparison table showing it as superior to generic SerpAPI
wrappers in setup complexity, maintenance overhead, and
AI-specific optimisation. The companion piece to Chan's
**google-news-mcp** server (first commit one day earlier,
2024-12-30) — together these two represent **one of the
earliest public sets of locale-aware MCP servers**, predating
most enterprise MCP adoption by months.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the locale-agnostic parameter handling,
authored every translation table, wrote the multi-channel
distribution pipeline (npm + Smithery + Docker + .mcpb), and
maintains the project as part of the matched Google News /
Google Jobs MCP pair. The Skywork review specifically
attributes the production-ready design and intelligent
error-handling architecture to Chan as creator.

projects.name: tencent-meeting-video-downloader
projects.tagline: Chrome extension for Tencent Meeting recordings
projects.publicSummary:
Chrome Manifest V3 extension that auto-detects Tencent Meeting
recording streams from the myqcloud.com CDN, generates an
optimised curl command (with automatic Windows-to-Unix path
conversion for Git Bash) and lets non-technical users save the
file locally with one click. 19 stars, 3 forks, listed on Chrome
Web Store.

narrative.problem:
Tencent Meeting recordings live on the myqcloud.com CDN behind
headers and URL patterns that make manual download brittle.
Educators, business operators, and content creators frequently
need to keep a local copy of a recorded session — for accessibility
captioning, archiving, or republishing to a different platform
— but the official UI offers no clean download path, and the
URL pattern is non-obvious enough that ad-hoc curl attempts
usually fail mid-stream.
narrative.audience:
The README explicitly names three groups: **educators** (recorded
lectures), **business professionals** (recorded meetings), and
**content creators** (recorded interviews / panels needing
local post-production). Secondary audience: any Mandarin-speaking
user in a corporate environment standardised on Tencent Meeting
who lacks IT permission to install desktop downloaders.
narrative.productLogic:
The extension sits passively in the browser and listens for
Tencent Meeting recording playback. When it detects a recording
stream, it surfaces the matched video in the popup interface
and offers a one-click "generate curl" action. The user types
(or pastes) a save path; the extension produces a ready-to-run
curl command with the right headers, the correct URL, and an
automatically converted Unix-style path for Git Bash. The
user pastes that into a terminal — no manual URL extraction,
no header guessing.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**Chrome Manifest V3** architecture using the **webRequest**
API to intercept the streaming response from
`*.myqcloud.com`. The interesting engineering is in the curl
generation: rather than emitting a naive URL, the extension
**strips problematic request headers** (the ones Tencent's
servers reject when re-issued from outside the browser
context) and **rewrites Windows paths to Unix-style** so the
command works in Git Bash — Chinese corporate environments
skew Windows-heavy, and Git Bash is the de-facto Unix shell
most users have. Built with JavaScript ES6+, Webpack 5, Babel.
narrative.outcomes:
**19 GitHub stars, 3 forks**, 24 commits, 2 releases — latest
v1.1.0 in December 2024. Listed on the **Chrome Web Store**
(user ratings visible on the listing). The user base is
primarily Mandarin-speaking and the README is structured
accordingly. Sustained traction for an explicitly niche
utility — the kind of project that only exists because Chan
used Tencent Meeting daily during the China-based work
portion of her career and wrote the tool she herself needed.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Reverse-engineered the myqcloud.com header
requirements, built the Chrome Manifest V3 extension, and
designed the cross-platform curl generation logic. Maintains
the project at v1.1.0 with periodic header updates as
Tencent's API evolves.

projects.name: gradient-svg-generator
projects.tagline: 340+ animated SVG templates — powers every visual on this profile
projects.publicSummary: 340+ animated gradient SVG banners delivered as URL-parameterised streaming SVG — paste a single `` link, no install required. Powers every visual on this profile. 273 commits, Vercel-deployed, CI via GitHub Actions, Vitest test suite.

narrative.problem:
Adding a striking animated banner to a GitHub profile or
documentation site requires either bespoke SVG authorship,
third-party paid services, or static images that look dated
within months. There is no zero-install service that lets a
developer drop a parameterised URL into Markdown and get a
live, animated, on-brand banner that updates without
re-rendering.
narrative.audience:
Developers, open-source maintainers, technical writers, and
community organisers who want their READMEs, profiles, and
landing pages to differentiate visually without dependency
sprawl. Specific subgroups served by template categories:
pride communities (20 dedicated pride-flag templates),
DevOps/full-stack practitioners (30 GitHub Profile templates),
creative technologists (29 experimental dimensional-portal
templates).
narrative.productLogic:
Single-URL contract: append parameters (`text=`, `template=`,
`height=`, `duration=`, plus colour stops `color0=`, `color1=`,
...) to the API endpoint, embed that URL as a Markdown image,
done. Users who don't want to handwrite parameters use the
`/create` visual editor — a GUI composer that previews changes
live and produces the final URL for copy-paste.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The service streams **dynamic SVG with embedded CSS animations**
rather than rasterising images — every banner is a small, live
XML document the browser animates locally, so file sizes stay
tiny and animations stay sharp at any zoom. Template
organisation is the architectural bet: 340+ templates split
into gradient types (horizontal / vertical / holographic /
radial), animation effects (morphing shapes, fluid dynamics,
consciousness streams), text rendering (path animation, glitch
effects), and background patterns (quantum tunnels,
electromagnetic fields, memory fragments) — each template a
self-contained parametric generator rather than a monolithic
switch. CI/CD: GitHub Actions; tests: Vitest; deploy: Vercel.
narrative.outcomes:
273 sustained commits. Production-grade availability via
Vercel's global edge. Apache-licensed for broad reuse. **Powers
every section header and visual badge in this very profile** —
the project is its own dogfooding evidence: if you can see
animated gradient banners on the README that generated this
page, you're looking at gradient-svg-generator output.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the URL-parameterised streaming-SVG
contract, authored every template category, built the live
builder at `/create`, and wired the Vercel + GitHub Actions
+ Vitest production pipeline. Uses the service across every
personal repo as continuous integration: any rendering
regression breaks visible branding immediately.

projects.name: FemTracker Agent
projects.tagline: 8-agent CopilotKit + LangGraph women's-health companion (merged into CopilotKit's official demos_2025)
projects.publicSummary:
AI-powered women's-health companion built on CopilotKit and Python
LangGraph, with **8 specialized agents** (Main Coordinator + Cycle
Tracker + Fertility Tracker + Symptom Mood + Nutrition Guide + Exercise
Coach + Lifestyle Manager + Health Insights) coordinating through
intelligent routing. WHO-standard health scoring (0-100 metric scales),
Redis caching with reported 90%+ hit rates, Supabase PostgreSQL
persistence. Merged into CopilotKit's official `demos_2025` showcase
via [PR #2068](https://github.com/CopilotKit/CopilotKit/pull/2068)
(publicly thanked by maintainer Tyler Slaton).

narrative.problem:
Existing women's-health apps treat menstrual cycle, fertility,
symptoms, nutrition, exercise, and lifestyle as **disconnected
verticals** — each one a separate dashboard, no shared model of
the user, no agent to reason across them. Users asking
"should I push my workout today given where I am in my cycle?"
get four sub-apps with no answer. Add the privacy concerns of
sensitive health data being aggregated by ad-funded apps and
the category is overdue for an agent-native rebuild.
narrative.audience:
Health-conscious women managing cycle-related symptoms,
planning conception, or pursuing holistic wellness — and the
secondary audience of agent-framework practitioners studying
what production-grade multi-agent coordination looks like
under HIPAA-aligned constraints. CopilotKit's maintainers
themselves are an audience: the project shipped into their
official `demos_2025` showcase.
narrative.productLogic:
Single conversational surface (CopilotKit) routes the user's
intent to one of eight specialised agents: **Main Coordinator**
+ **Cycle Tracker** + **Fertility Tracker** + **Symptom Mood**
+ **Nutrition Guide** + **Exercise Coach** + **Lifestyle
Manager** + **Health Insights**. Each agent has its own
domain logic and prompt scaffolding but shares the same
Supabase-backed user model, so a question that crosses two
domains ("am I dehydrated today?", which touches nutrition +
cycle + exercise) gets routed through the Coordinator and
answered with WHO-standard scoring rather than three
disconnected guesses.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The architectural bet is **CopilotKit on the frontend +
LangGraph on the backend**, communicating through a CopilotKit
runtime adapter. Next.js 15 + React 19 + TypeScript on the
frontend; Python 3.12 + LangGraph for agent orchestration;
Supabase PostgreSQL with **Row Level Security** for
privacy-preserving multi-tenant persistence; Redis caching
achieving 90%+ hit rates on hot read paths. The health-scoring
engine implements WHO-standard algorithms across six dimensions:
exercise (150 min/week benchmark), hydration (2 000 mL daily),
cycle regularity (21–35 day window), mood patterns, lifestyle
factors, fertility indicators — each 0–100, composited into
actionable insights. Codebase is 83.2% TypeScript for type
safety in a medical-domain product.
narrative.outcomes:
**Merged into CopilotKit's official `demos_2025` showcase**
via [PR #2068](https://github.com/CopilotKit/CopilotKit/pull/2068),
publicly thanked by maintainer Tyler Slaton — external
recognition of production-grade quality from the framework's
own maintainers, not just a self-published demo. Live demo at
femtracker-agent.vercel.app: reported 95+ Lighthouse score,
sub-second TTFB, 99.9% uptime. Demo video at the linked YouTube
URL. 90 sustained commits.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author of the agent architecture, the WHO-scoring engine,
the Supabase schema with Row Level Security, and the CopilotKit
runtime adapter. Drove the project into CopilotKit's official
demo set — a path that required not just shipping but
demonstrating reference-implementation-grade quality to a
third-party maintainer team.

projects.publicSummary:
A **no-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API**, built
so that **non-technical users never have to read Resend's docs,
open Resend's dashboard, or learn merge-field syntax** to send
beautiful personalised email campaigns. Started life on Christmas
Eve 2025 as a one-shot CLI for sending Christmas greetings; four
days later, on Dec 27 2025, the codebase pivoted in a single
commit (`fe16fdd`: *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*) into a full Next.js 15 web app with a
drag-and-drop block-based template editor (7 block types,
`@dnd-kit`), a 5-step sending wizard, a Resend Audiences sync,
and a deliberately authored **onboarding + context-aware help
system "for non-technical users"** (commit `782731c`). Wraps four
Resend API surfaces — `/emails`, `/templates`, `/audiences`, and
`/audiences/.../contacts` — behind a unified UI so the user
never sees a request body. Ships **6 Neobrutalism preset
templates**, real-time desktop/mobile preview, undo/redo with
50-state history, and CSV-import contact management.
Privacy-first: all user data (templates, contacts, settings)
lives in browser localStorage, and the Resend API key is passed
per-request rather than persisted on any server. Live at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/),
migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 with
GitHub Actions auto-deploy.

narrative.problem:
Resend is the modern transactional-email API of choice for
developers — clean SDK, fast onboarding, MX-record sanity — but
the gap it leaves wide open is **non-technical users**.
Marketers, community managers, school teachers, holiday-card
senders, small-business owners, and event organisers want
exactly what Resend provides (high deliverability, real
sender domains, real bounce handling) but **cannot get
through the API on-ramp**. To send one campaign through
vanilla Resend, a non-technical user has to (1) read SDK docs,
(2) write Node code with the `Resend` client, (3) hand-author
HTML or React Email JSX, (4) understand merge-field syntax
differences between Resend's native templates and their own
templating, (5) manage `Audiences` and `Contacts` through the
REST API directly, and (6) deploy a serverless function or
run scripts locally. Every one of these steps is a wall.
Tools that **do** cater to non-technical users — Mailchimp,
ConvertKit, Substack — are either expensive at small list
sizes, lock the user into their templating, or don't expose
the underlying mail infrastructure (custom domains, API key
rotation, deliverability metrics) that Resend gives away
cheaply.
narrative.audience:
Explicitly **non-technical or semi-technical Resend users**.
The persona stack: (a) marketers and community managers who
want to send a polished campaign through Resend's
deliverability stack but don't write Next.js; (b) holiday
senders who want to send Christmas / Lunar New Year / Birthday
greetings to dozens of friends with personalised names baked
in; (c) small-business owners running newsletters out of
their own domain who don't want Mailchimp branding or pricing;
(d) developers who *can* code but want a visual editor for
speed because they're authoring one-off campaigns rather than
building a templated system. Secondary audience: other
developers who want to study a worked example of wrapping a
commercial API into a no-code platform — the codebase ships
MIT-licensed as a study artefact.
narrative.productLogic:
The product replaces six pieces of Resend's developer
workflow with visual surfaces. **(1) HTML authoring** becomes
a drag-and-drop block editor with 7 block types — Header,
Text, Image, Button, Wishes List, Divider, Footer — each with
a focused property panel; React Email renders to real
email-client-safe HTML on the server. **(2) Templating** is
the visual canvas plus a theme panel with 9+ colour pickers
(`react-colorful`), 600px/375px device preview toggle, and
50-state undo/redo. **(3) Recipient management** becomes a
Contacts page with three import modes — manual entry, CSV
bulk import (Email/FirstName/LastName columns), and a
one-click sync from a Resend Audience (`audienceId` selected
from a dropdown). **(4) Merge fields** become four named
variables — `{{recipientName}}`, `{{senderName}}`,
`{{currentYear}}`, `{{nextYear}}` — with the server-side
renderer normalising between local camelCase syntax and
Resend native UPPER_SNAKE_CASE so a template can use either.
**(5) Sending** becomes a 5-step wizard: pick template -> pick
recipients -> customise variables -> preview the actual
rendered HTML (not a mockup) -> send-and-track with a real
per-recipient success/failure result table; the server hits
Resend per recipient with progress streamed back. **(6) API
infrastructure** is replaced by a Settings page where the
user pastes their Resend API key and verified sender email
once; the key never leaves the browser except as a
per-request header. A welcome-modal **onboarding flow**
(commit `782731c`, *"comprehensive onboarding and help
system for non-technical users"*) walks first-time visitors
through these six surfaces step by step, and every screen
has a context-aware help modal pinned to it.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source under MIT. The product is **free to self-host**
(deploy to Cloudflare Workers in under a minute via the
`npm run deploy` script and `@opennextjs/cloudflare`) and
free to use on the public instance at
sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev (the user supplies their own
Resend API key, so Chan bears no email-delivery cost). The
commercial premise is asymmetric: Resend already monetises
through their own free tier (3,000 emails/month) plus paid
plans for higher volume; SendJoy makes their funnel reachable
to a customer segment Resend can't service directly, which
makes it a complementary product rather than a competing
one. The build-in-public value for Chan: a portfolio-grade
worked example of **"abstract a developer API into a no-code
product"** — a pattern the agentic-AI economy will need
repeatedly as more APIs ship without UX layers. The codebase
is deliberately reusable as a template for wrapping any
other Resend-shaped API (transactional SMS, push
notifications, file delivery).
narrative.technicalApproach:
Next.js 15.1.2 App Router with React 19 and TypeScript 5.7,
deployed on **Cloudflare Workers via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`** (migrated from Vercel on
2026-02-04, commit `e9ace8a`, with a follow-up GitHub
Actions auto-deploy workflow in commit `a970c1a`). Four
Resend-wrapping API routes form the entire backend surface:
**`POST /api/send`** takes `{apiKey, from, to, subject,
html}` and forwards to the Resend SDK, returning
`{success, data: {id}, error?}`; **`POST /api/preview`**
takes `{blocks, theme, variables}` and runs
`@react-email/render` to produce email-client-safe HTML
with merge fields substituted; **`GET/POST/DELETE
/api/contacts`** wraps the Resend Audiences contact CRUD;
**`GET /api/resend-templates`** plus its `[id]/duplicate`
and `[id]/publish` sub-routes wrap Resend's cloud
templates. **State management** uses Zustand 5.0 with a
50-state undo/redo history stack — every block move, edit,
delete, or theme change is reversible. **Templates and
contacts persist to browser localStorage** under six
namespaced keys (`email-platform-templates`,
`email-platform-contacts`, `email-platform-settings`,
`email-platform-onboarding`, `email-platform-emails-sent`,
`email-platform-sidebar-collapsed`) — there is no
server-side database, no auth layer, and no telemetry. The
editor itself uses **`@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable`**
for accessible drag-and-drop (commit `1dadb76`, *"implement
drag-and-drop block reordering"*). The visual language is a
custom **Neobrutalism design system** — 4px solid black
borders, 8px hard-offset shadows (no blur), three brand
colours (`#DC2626` red, `#16A34A` green, `#F59E0B` gold),
custom Tailwind `shadow-neo-*` utilities — that signals
"approachable / playful" while still feeling polished.
Performance: 95+ Lighthouse, <1s TTFB, <100ms API. **GEO
optimisation** (commit `04fc131`, *"add Generative Engine
Optimization for AI discoverability"*) ships
machine-readable metadata for LLM-driven discovery, same
pattern as Chan's other public projects.
narrative.outcomes:
Live and functional at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/)
since end of December 2025; 43 solo commits across roughly
6 weeks of active development with subsequent maintenance
through April 2026. The defining engineering moment is the
**4-day arc from CLI to visual platform**: Dec 23 ships
commit `16c7026` *"initial release of Christmas greeting
email sender"* (a CLI script using `tsx` and `dotenv` to
send one templated greeting via Resend), Dec 27 ships
commit `fe16fdd` *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*, and Dec 27–28 layers the visual
editor, the 5-step wizard, the Resend cloud-templates
integration, the Resend Audiences sync, and the onboarding
flow for non-technical users on top — all before the
product gets its final name `send-joy` (commit `dba12bc`,
Dec 29) and brand identity (`0798540`). The Feb 2026
Cloudflare Workers migration cuts hosting cost to zero and
adds GitHub Actions auto-deploy. The codebase serves as
Chan's worked example of the "wrap a developer API into a
no-code product for non-technical users" pattern — directly
cited in conversations with founders and recruiters as a
demonstration of full-stack product judgement (not just
engineering): identifying the non-technical-user gap in a
developer-first API, then closing it in a week.
narrative.myRole:
Sole product designer, sole developer, sole maintainer. Made
the call to pivot from a one-off Christmas CLI into a
general-purpose visual platform after the CLI's 6th commit;
designed the Neobrutalism visual language; designed the
7-block schema; designed the 5-step wizard; designed the
onboarding flow specifically for non-technical users (the
explicit framing in commit `782731c`); implemented every
Resend API wrapper route; engineered the localStorage
persistence model with zero server-side state; executed the
Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers migration with GitHub Actions
auto-deploy; authored the README, TUTORIAL.md, and GEO
metadata. Maintains the live deployment today.
